1: Chapter 12 opens the 'Second Burden' (massa), which spans chapters 12-14. The superscription 'the word of the LORD concerning Israel' is unusual: the preceding oracles (chs 9-11) were about the nations; this oracle specifically targets Israel/Jerusalem. The creator-credentials: 'who stretched out the heavens and founded the earth and formed the spirit of man within him' — YHWH's authority to judge and restore Jerusalem is grounded in his cosmic creatorship. The creator of heaven and earth can reshape the destiny of his city.
2: 'Behold, I am about to make Jerusalem a cup of staggering to all the surrounding peoples. The siege of Jerusalem will also be against Judah.' The image of Jerusalem as a cup of drunkenness (saf ra'al, H7196 + H7478 — a cup/basin that makes the nations reel/stagger) is the reversal of Isa 51:17-23 (the cup of YHWH's wrath given to Jerusalem is taken back and given to her enemies). The nations that attack Jerusalem will drink and stagger — the city that appeared vulnerable will prove to be an intoxicating trap for her attackers.
3: 'On that day I will make Jerusalem a heavy stone for all the peoples. All who lift it will surely hurt themselves. And all the nations of the earth will gather against it.' A heavy stone (even ma'amasah) that injures all who try to lift or carry it — an image of irresistible weight. This directly addresses the Zechariah-era context: the small, returning community in Jerusalem appears to be easy pickings for the surrounding nations. YHWH promises that Jerusalem will be the stone that breaks the nations who attempt to remove or conquer it. The gathering of all nations against Jerusalem is the eschatological Armageddon (Rev 16:16; 19:19).
4: 'On that day, declares the LORD, I will strike every horse with panic, and its rider with madness. But for the sake of the house of Judah I will keep my eyes open, when I strike every horse of the peoples with blindness.' YHWH will strike the nations' cavalry with three afflictions: timmahon (panic, bewilderment, H8541), shigga'on (madness, H7697), and ivvaron (blindness, H5788). The contrast: while the horses and riders of the nations are struck with panic and blindness, YHWH's eyes remain open for the house of Judah. Divine attention is the protection: YHWH who neither slumbers nor sleeps (Ps 121:4) keeps watch over his people while striking their attackers with blindness.
5: 'Then the clans of Judah shall say to themselves, The inhabitants of Jerusalem have strength through the LORD of hosts, their God.' The recognition by Judah's clans (alufei Yehudah, the clan leaders of Judah outside Jerusalem) that the city's survival is YHWH's doing, not human fortification. The acknowledgment that strength comes 'through YHWH of hosts, their God' (bADONAI tzvaot eloheihem) is the theological center of the reversal: the restoration of covenant language ('their God') accompanying the restoration of covenant protection.
6: 'On that day I will make the clans of Judah like a blazing pot in the midst of wood, like a flaming torch among sheaves. And they shall devour to the right and to the left all the surrounding peoples, while Jerusalem shall again be inhabited in its place, in Jerusalem.' The clan leaders of Judah become like fire among kindling — an irresistible, spreading, consuming force. The metaphor of fire among dry wood is the most powerful image of devastating effectiveness. Jerusalem, paradoxically, will be inhabited in its place while Judah's clans consume the surrounding nations — the center is secure while the perimeter is aggressive.
7: 'And the LORD will give salvation to the tents of Judah first, that the glory of the house of David and the glory of the inhabitants of Jerusalem may not surpass that of Judah.' The order of salvation: the rural clans (tents of Judah) first, then the city (Jerusalem). This prevents pride: if the city were saved first and the rural areas afterward, Jerusalem and the Davidic house might claim superiority over Judah's villages. YHWH deliberately orders salvation to prevent hierarchical pride within the covenant community.
8: 'On that day the LORD will protect the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so that the feeblest among them on that day shall be like David, and the house of David shall be like God, like the angel of the LORD, going before them.' The escalating empowerment: the weakest person in Jerusalem becomes David-like (David = the standard of warrior courage and YHWH-dependence); the house of David becomes like God (ke'Elohim), like the Angel of the LORD who went before Israel (Exod 23:20-23). The Angel of the LORD who led the Exodus is the same divine figure who now, in the eschatological battle, leads the Davidic house. The equation of 'like God' and 'like the Angel of the LORD' in the same phrase is a Christological prolepsis.
9: 'And on that day I will seek to destroy all the nations that come against Jerusalem.' The divine warrior's purpose stated directly: avaqesh lehashmid (I will seek to destroy) all the nations attacking Jerusalem. The nations who come against YHWH's city come against YHWH himself; their destruction is the vindication of the city and the covenant. Verses 10-14 immediately follow with the pouring out of the spirit of grace and the mourning for the pierced one — the judgment and the salvation occur simultaneously.
10: 'And I will pour out on the house of David and the inhabitants of Jerusalem a spirit of grace and pleas for mercy, so that, when they look on me, on him whom they have pierced, they shall mourn for him, as one mourns for an only child, and weep bitterly over him, as one weeps over a firstborn.' Zechariah 12:10 is one of the most theologically rich verses in the OT. The spirit of grace and supplication (ruach chen vetachanunin) is poured out — a new covenant act (cf. Ezek 36:26-27; Joel 2:28-29). The grammatical anomaly: 'they shall look to me whom they have pierced' — YHWH is the one pierced, yet the text shifts from 'me' to 'him' in the same sentence. John 19:37 cites this at the crucifixion; Rev 1:7 applies it to the parousia. The mourning is as intense as mourning for an only child (yachid) and a firstborn (bechor) — the most devastating losses in the ancient world. The mourning represents both repentance (Acts 2:37) and grief over what was done to the Messiah.
11: 'On that day the mourning in Jerusalem will be as great as the mourning for Hadad-rimmon in the plain of Megiddo.' The comparison to the mourning for Hadad-rimmon (H1910 — possibly a location in the Jezreel Valley, or a deity associated with a mourning ritual) in the plain of Megiddo links to the mourning for King Josiah, who was mortally wounded there (2 Chr 35:24-25). The national mourning for Josiah — the greatest reforming king, cut off in his prime — was the most intense collective grief in Israel's recent memory. The eschatological mourning over the pierced one will be even greater.
12: 'The land shall mourn, each family by itself: the family of the house of David by itself, and their wives by itself; the family of the house of Nathan by itself, and their wives by itself.' The mourning is organized by households, with men and women mourning separately — the pattern of Yom Kippur worship (the most solemn day of the Jewish calendar). The four families listed (David, Nathan, Levi, Shimei) represent royal, prophetic, priestly, and Levitical lineages — the comprehensive range of Israel's covenant leadership all united in this mourning. Nathan the son of David (2 Sam 5:14) represents the non-Solomonic branch of the Davidic line, important for the NT genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:31).
13: 'The family of the house of Levi by itself, and their wives by itself; the family of the Shimeites by itself, and their wives by itself.' The Levitical and Shimeite (a Levitical clan descended from Shimei son of Gershon, Num 3:21) families add the priestly class to the universal mourning. The separation of husbands and wives in mourning reflects both the privacy of intense grief and perhaps the gender-separated worship practice of the temple. The comprehensive social representation — royal, prophetic, priestly, Levitical — ensures that no class escapes or stands aloof from the communal mourning.
14: 'And all the remaining families, each by itself, and their wives by themselves.' The all-inclusive conclusion: kol hamishpachot hanisharot (all the remaining families) — every family in the land joins the mourning. The mourning is truly universal: no household, clan, tribe, or class is exempt. The national mourning over the pierced one represents the moment when the entire covenant community comes to recognize the identity of the one they rejected.
